attributes.stem:
A 70-year-old man presents with a tender swelling in his anterior aspect of his left knee. Aside from cleaning the floor while kneeling down yesterday, there was no history of trauma. The left knee presented with mild pain on movement. Further history reveals that he is a retired coal miner with an active lifestyle and no other significant history.

On examination, he is afebrile with no obvious signs of distress. The swelling can be felt inferior to the patella, with some bruising posteriorly (Image). The swelling is warm to the touch and is fluctuant. While there is a slight decrease in range of movement, he is still able to mobilise without any difficulty.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This is a classic presentation for infrapatellar bursitis, also known as “clergyman’s” or “housemaid’s” knee. It is precipitated by long periods of kneeling and generally resolves with the principles of rest, ice, compression, and elevation (RICE) management for soft tissues injuries. Very rarely aspiration, corticosteroid injection or surgery would be needed.

Investigations are generally not needed, but might be indicated in the following circumstances:

  • enlarged bursa on ultrasound

  • <1500 wbc on bursal fluid analysis

  • >=0.5 serum glucose ratio on bursal fluid analysis

  • clear cloudy serosanguineous fluid

Unnecessary analysis does increase the risk of iatrogenic septic bursitis and is not recommended unless a septic bursitis is suspected.

Septic bursitis is quite uncommon. When sepsis is present, it will usually be associated with significant pain, tenderness and a decreased range of motion. It is often more common in those who are immunocompromised. If sepsis is present, antibiotic coverage of staph aureus (80-85% of cause) and strep pyogenes should be considered along with aspiration of the bursa with fluid analysis.

Patella dislocation is usually associated with either a direct blow to the knee, or a sudden twisting action which displaces the patella laterally. Recurrence rate ranges from 15-60%. Some anatomical variations like patella alta and trochlear dysplasia acna make it more likely to get patellar dislocation along with people with generalised ligamentous laxity. Overall, the patella is often dislocated laterally due to the quadricep pull. An individual (particularly women) with a wider Q angle mean that they are predisposed to having a dislocation. Medial dislocations are rare and are more often due to quadricep atrophy, iatrogenic or a congenital cause. The knee will feel like it gave way with a "pop" feeling and sometimes an acute swelling. There might be joint effusion or haemarthrosis seen. Some patients also exhibit a J sign, where there is excessive patellar glide. Management is done by prompt reduction and analgesics where necessary. Physiotherapy is needed for long term benefits.

Patellofemoral syndrome, aka runners' knee, is seen in young sportspeople and the elderly. It involves anterior knee pain which is aggravated by activities such as squatting or climbing stairs. It is mainly a diagnosis of exclusion with no clear aetiological explanation although mal-alignment and muscle imbalance is the leading explanation. Conservative management is often sufficient.

Osteoarthritis is a common multifactorial degenerative disorder that often can be managed conservatively and analgesics. Severe osteoarthritis may require surgery. These cases will often present with joint pain, stiffness, swelling and if severe enough mal-alignment.
learningPoints: Physical examination and history is sufficient to diagnose a case of bursitis.
references: Chatra PS. Bursae around the knee joints. The Indian Journal of Radiology & Imaging. 2012 Mar 1;22(1).
KHODAEE M. Common Superficial Bursitis. American Family Physician. 2017 Feb 15;95(4):224–31.

maffulli  nicola, longo  umile, denaro  vincenzo. Bursitis - Symptoms, diagnosis and treatment [Internet]. BMJ Best Practice. 2019 [cited 2023 Feb 20]. Available from: https://bestpractice.bmj.com/topics/en-gb/523
